title: Stuffed bell peppers
subtitle: Roasted peppers filled with seasoned beef, rice, and tomato sauce
excerpt: Whole red or yellow peppers, tops removed and seeds cleared, filled with a mixture of cooked long-grain rice, beef mince, onion, and tinned tomatoes, then baked until the pepper is tender and slightly charred and the filling is cooked through. A complete meal in a vegetable.
sourceNotes: Stuffed pepper preparations appear across Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and Central European cooking traditions. The American version with minced beef and rice became a mid-twentieth-century domestic staple through its inclusion in home economics teaching and its adaptability to whatever minced meat and leftover rice were on hand. Similar preparations appear in Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked stuffed peppers individually. Reheat from frozen, covered with foil, at 180°C fan for 35 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made up to 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Fill the peppers and bake just before serving.

content.text: Choose peppers that stand upright on their own: a three- or four-lobed base is more stable than a two-lobed one. Red and yellow peppers are sweeter than green once roasted, and they soften to a better texture in the oven time available. If you use green peppers, add 10 minutes to the baking time as they take slightly longer to become tender.

The filling contains already-cooked rice. Using raw rice leads to uneven cooking — the rice may still be firm when the pepper is done. Cook the rice first (leftover rice works perfectly here), then mix it into the browned meat and tomato mixture before filling. Season the filling more assertively than you think necessary; pepper walls dilute flavour.

items.prepNote: tops sliced off and reserved, seeds removed

content.text: Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Brown the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince in a saucepan over medium-high heat, breaking it up, for 5 to 6 minutes. Add the {{onion}} and {{garlic}} and cook for 4 minutes more. Add the {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika and {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, season generously with salt and pepper, and simmer for 8 to 10 minutes until slightly thickened. Remove from the heat and stir in the cooked {{long-grain-rice}} of rice.

Stand the {{pepper-red}} peppers upright in a baking dish. Spoon the filling into each pepper, packing it down firmly and mounding it slightly above the rim. If using cheddar, scatter {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar over the top of each pepper. Replace the pepper lids or leave them off for a more caramelised top. Add a splash of water to the base of the dish. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the peppers are tender and slightly charred at the edges.

items.symptom: Peppers are still firm and watery after baking
items.cause: Not baked long enough, or oven temperature too low
items.fix: Return for a further 10 minutes; the pepper walls should collapse slightly and char lightly at the edges when done

items.cause: Too little sauce in the filling, or overcooked before filling
items.fix: The tomato mixture should be loose enough to look slightly wet before the rice is added; the rice will absorb liquid as it bakes

items.symptom: Peppers fall over in the oven
items.cause: Unstable base shape, or dish too large
items.fix: Choose even-based peppers and use a dish just large enough to hold them upright; they can lean against each other without harm

items.cause: Under-seasoned; the pepper wall dilutes flavour
items.fix: Season the filling more assertively than a sauce you would serve directly — it needs to carry through the pepper

content.text: Stuffed peppers appear in virtually every cuisine that uses both sweet peppers and minced meat — Central European, Turkish, Spanish, Italian, Middle Eastern. The American version is a mid-twentieth-century home-cooking standard, probably descended from Central European immigrant cooking as much as from any native American tradition, and it became a fixture on the weekly rotation of American home cooks because it uses cheap ingredients efficiently, requires minimal skill, and produces a complete meal in a single vessel. It is a dish that repays careful seasoning and almost nothing else.
